corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: We hypothesize that this is a limitation of our decoder hierarchy producing an image at a base resolution of 64 × 64 and then upsampling it.

We design our generative stack to produce images from captions using two components: • A prior P (z i |y) that produces CLIP image embeddings z i conditioned on captions y. • A decoder P (x|z i , y) that produces images x conditioned on CLIP image embeddings z i (and optionally text captions y).
To automate aesthetic quality evaluations, we follow the procedure used by Crowson , training a linear regression model on images and mean ratings from the AVA dataset .

To leverage these representations for image generation, we propose a two-stage model: a prior that generates a CLIP image embedding given a text caption, and a decoder that generates an image conditioned on the image embedding.
Within this framework, CLIP has emerged as a successful representation learner for images.

CLIP embeddings have a number of desirable properties: they are robust to image distribution shift, have impressive zero-shot capabilities, and have been fine-tuned to achieve state-of-the-art results on a wide variety of vision and language tasks .
Concurrently, diffusion models have emerged as a promising generative modeling framework, pushing the state-of-the-art on image and video generation tasks .
Concurrently, diffusion models have emerged as a promising generative modeling framework, pushing the state-of-the-art on image and video generation tasks .

Preechakul et al. propose an autoencoder framework where diffusion models are used to render latent variables as images, and a second diffusion model is used to generate these latents (similar to our diffusion prior).

Preechakul et al. propose an autoencoder framework where diffusion models are used to render latent variables as images, and a second diffusion model is used to generate these latents (similar to our diffusion prior).
For diffusion models, Dhariwal and Nichol introduced classifier guidance as a way to use gradients from a classifier trained on noised images to steer the model towards higher quality generations.

While the diffusion models themselves cannot generate images unconditionally, the authors experimented with a simple approach for two-stage image generation by employing Kernel Density Estimation to sample image representations.
Our training dataset consists of pairs (x, y) of images x and their corresponding captions y.
For diversity, we propose a new evaluation protocol in which humans are presented with two 4 × 4 grids of samples and must choose which is more diverse (with a third option, "Not sure").
More research is also needed on how the change in architecture changes how the model learns biases in training data.
We present our results in Table .
